lines.target: But literature is not just made up of names from the past: the literary tradition is in good health, and authors are writing their works in English all over the world.
lines.base: Pero la literatura no está compuesta de nombres del pasado: la tradición literaria goza de buena salud, y autores que escriben sus obras en inglés en todo el mundo. (5)

lines.target: Why do the British have such a long-standing tradition of writers, while at the same time having a reputation for being reserved and reticent to discuss emotions? That is perhaps the reason!
lines.base: ¿Por qué tienen los británicos una tan larga tradición de escritores, mientras que al mismo tiempo tienen fama reservados y reticentes para discutir sus emociones? ¡Eso es quizás la razón! (6)

lines.target: An English writer of Hungarian origin described how men from different countries declare their love for a woman:
lines.base: Un escritor inglés de origen húngaro describió cómo los hombres de los distintos países declaran su amor a una mujer:

lines.target: A Frenchman will go down on his knees and say — Darling, I love you, I want to marry you.
lines.base: Un francés se pondrá de rodillas y dirá: — Querida, te amo, quiero casarme contigo.

lines.target: A Russian, too, will go down on his knees and declare — If you don’t marry me, I will blow my brains out!
lines.base: Un ruso también se pondrá de rodillas y declarará: — Si no te casas conmigo, ¡me haré saltar la tapa de los sesos! (6)

lines.target: Whereas an Englishman, consumed by passion, will say — I say, why don’t we...?
lines.base: Mientras que un inglés, devorado por la pasión, dirá: — Y si nosotros...? (7)

lines.target: Fortunately, not all English literature is concerned with such matters.
lines.base: Afortunadamente, la literatura inglesa no se preocupa únicamente de tales cuestiones.

lines.target: Great writing transforms the minds of readers and leads them to a fuller understanding of humanity,
lines.base: La gran literatura transforma las mentes de los lectores y los lleva a una comprensión más acabada de la humanidad,

lines.target: although there are some more cynical observers who suggest that literature used to be an art and finance a profession
lines.base: aunque existen algunos observadores más cínicos que sugieren que la literatura era en otros tiempos un arte y las finanzas una profesión,

lines.target: whereas today the opposite is very often true: paperback rights for a popular novelist
lines.base: mientras que hoy en día lo contrario es muy a menudo la verdad: los derechos del libro de bolsillo para un novelista de moda

lines.target: sell for millions of dollars, and book stands at airports and stations are crammed with pulp fiction.
lines.base: que se venden por millones de dólares, y los kioskos en los aeropuertos y las estaciones están repletos de obras de ficción que no valen un cuarto.

lines.target: However, we would hate you to become like a professor of English who was very erudite
lines.base: Sin embargo, nos disgustaría que se convirtiera usted en cierto profesor de inglés muy erudito,

lines.target: but of whom his students used to say — He knows everything about literature... except how to enjoy it. (N4)
lines.base: de quien sus estudiantes decían: — Lo sabe todo acerca de la literatura... excepto cómo disfrutarla.

lines.target: One of the great pleasures of mastering a language is to be able to read writers in the original.
lines.base: Uno de los placeres mayores de dominar un idioma es el de ser capaz de leer a los autores en las versiones originales.

lines.target: In the next few lessons we will present you with some examples of the work of two twentieth-century authors,
lines.base: En las dos o tres lecciones siguientes le presentaremos algunos ejemplos de las obras de dos autores del siglo 20,

lines.target: and we hope you will not only appreciate the extracts but enjoy reading them, too.
lines.base: y esperamos que no solamente apreciará los pasajes, sino que disfrutará al leerlos también.

gap_candidates.target: But literature is not just made up of names from the past: the literary tradition is in good health, and authors are writing their works in English all over the world. (5)
Why do the British have such a long-standing tradition of writers, while at the same time having a reputation for being reserved and reticent to discuss emotions? That is perhaps the reason! (6)
An English writer of Hungarian origin described how men from different countries declare their love for a woman:
A Frenchman will go down on his knees and say — Darling, I love you, I want to marry you.
A Russian, too, will go down on his knees and declare — If you don’t marry me, I will blow my brains out! (6)
Whereas an Englishman, consumed by passion, will say — I say, why don’t we...? (7)
Fortunately, not all English literature is concerned with such matters.
Great writing transforms the minds of readers and leads them to a fuller understanding of humanity,
although there are some more cynical observers who suggest that literature used to be an art and finance a profession
whereas today the opposite is very often true: paperback rights for a popular novelist (8)
sell for millions of dollars, and book stands at airports and stations are crammed with pulp fiction. (9)
However, we would hate you to become like a professor of English who was very erudite (10)
but of whom his students used to say — He knows everything about literature... except how to enjoy it. (N4)
One of the great pleasures of mastering a language is to be able to read writers in the original.
In the next few lessons we will present you with some examples of the work of two twentieth-century authors,
and we hope you will not only appreciate the extracts but enjoy reading them, too.
